coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots originated from chicken blood as peroxidase mimics for colorimetric detection of biothiols
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Chunling
Qin, Xiu
Xu, Yuanjin
Li, Xiaoping
Chen, Yuye
Shi, Rui
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared from chicken blood by hydrothermal method, which could catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 to generate the blue oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with a strong absorption peak at 652 nm. However, the introduction of biothiols could induce the reduction of ox-TMB, leading to shallower blueness and lower absorbance. Based on this fact, a convenient and sensitive colorimetric method for detection of biothiols using CQDs as peroxidase mimics was established. Several factors, such as acidity, temperature, incubating time, CQDs dosage, H2O2 and TMB concentrations, which might influence the response of the analysis signal, were optimized by taking cysteine (CySH) as an example. Under the selected conditions, sensitive detection of biothiols was achieved, and the limits of detection (LOD) for CySH, glutathione (GSH), and homocysteine (HcySH) were 0.4, 0.6, and 0.9 μM respectively. This method was successfully applied for the determination of CySH in human serum samples with the recovery of 91.5 %–109.3 %, indicating the potential application of this method for biothiols sensing in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Colorimetry
Peroxidase mimics
Determination

Evaluation of peroxidase-like activity of CB-CQDs
Morphology and optical properties of CB-CQDs
Application of CB-CQDs in biothiols detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The present work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21567002 ), the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery ( GXKLB-201801 ) and the Foundation of College Student Innovation Ability Training of Guangxi University ( 201910593179 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Microstructure characterization of CB-CQDs: (a) TEM image, (b) particle size distribution, and (c) HRTEM image.
(a) The emission spectra of CB-CQDs excited at 300-440 nm. Inset: photograph of CB-CQDs aqueous solution under UV light (365 nm). (b) FTIR spectrum of CB-CQDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra of (a) TMB, (b) TMB + H2O2, and (c) TMB + H2O2 + CB-CQDs in HAc-NaAc buffer at pH 4.5. Inset: the corresponding photographs.

UV–vis spectra of (a) TMB + H2O2 + CB-CQDs + CySH, (b) TMB + H2O2 + CB-CQDs + GSH, (c) TMB + H2O2 + CB-CQDs + HcySH, and (d) TMB + H2O2 + CB-CQDs in HAc-NaAc buffer at pH 4.5. Inset: the corresponding photographs.

(a) UV–vis spectra of ox-TMB with various concentrations of CySH. From above to below, the concentrations of CySH are 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 40 μM, respectively. Inset: the corresponding photographs. (b) The linear relation of ΔA with concentrations of CySH in the range of 0.5 to 20 μM.
Selectivity of biothiols detection, the concentration of CySH, CySH and HcySH were 10 μM, the other amino acids concentration were 1 mM.
Sketch of the preparation of CQDs from chicken blood and the detection mechanism for biothiols.
Analytical performance of different nanoprobe for sensing of CySH.
Determination results of CySH in human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots originated from chicken blood as peroxidase mimics for colorimetric detection of biothiols

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery, Guangxi University, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared from chicken blood by hydrothermal method, which could catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in the presence of H2O2 to generate the blue oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) with a strong absorption peak at 652 nm. However, the introduction of biothiols could induce the reduction of ox-TMB, leading to shallower blueness and lower absorbance. Based on this fact, a convenient and sensitive colorimetric method for detection of biothiols using CQDs as peroxidase mimics was established. Several factors, such as acidity, temperature, incubating time, CQDs dosage, H2O2 and TMB concentrations, which might influence the response of the analysis signal, were optimized by taking cysteine (CySH) as an example. Under the selected conditions, sensitive detection of biothiols was achieved, and the limits of detection (LOD) for CySH, glutathione (GSH), and homocysteine (HcySH) were 0.4, 0.6, and 0.9 μM respectively. This method was successfully applied for the determination of CySH in human serum samples with the recovery of 91.5 %–109.3 %, indicating the potential application of this method for biothiols sensing in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine (CySH), glutathione (GSH) and homocysteine (HcySH) are sulfhydryl-containing amino acids known as biothiols, which are highly reductive and play important roles in maintaining normal physiological process [1–3]. The concentration of CySH in human plasma is between 50−300 μM [4]. The deficiency of CySH may result in muscular atrophy, weakness, and liver damage [5]. An increased level of plasma CySH can cause cardiovascular disease [6]. The content of GSH in human serum is maintained at 7−21 μM [7]. Abnormal levels of GSH are related to many diseases including heart problems, neurodegenerative diseases, and cancer [8]. The normal concentration of HcySH in human plasma is in the range of 5−15 μM [4]. High level of blood HcySH is involved in hypertension, coronary heart disease, myocardial infarction, etc [9,10]. Therefore, the detection of biothiols is of significant importance for human health. Various analytical techniques, such as electrochemistry [11,12], high performance liquid chromatography [13,14], chemiluminescence [15,16], fluorimetry [17–19], colorimetry [20,21], etc, have been applied for biothiols determination. In comparison with other analytical techniques, colorimetry is simple, practical and low cost [22–24]. Colorimetric method related to nanomaterials has been used for the detection of biothiols. Zhang's research group reported a convenient and sensitive method to determine trace CySH using pectinase protected gold nanoparticles as colorimetric probe [25]. Mohammadi and coworker developed a colorimetric method for biothiols assay based on the aggregation of silver nanoparticles [26].

Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), as a natural enzyme, possesses remarkable advantages such as excellent substrate specificities and high efficiency under mild conditions, which makes the HRP-based biosensors highly selective and responsive. HRP also has the disadvantages of high cost, poor stability, and easily denaturing in extreme conditions, which hinders its widespread applications. In recent years, considerable research efforts [27–29] have been done in exploration of peroxidase-like to replace HRP in the construction of enzymatic assays. A series of nanomaterials, including noble metals [30], transition metal oxides or chalcogenides [31,32], have been used as peroxidase mimetic for the detection of H2O2, glucose, etc. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), one of the most popular representatives of fluorescent nanomaterials [33], have been widely used in diverse fields ranging from fluorescent analysis to bioimaging and light emitting diode [34–36], due to their unique properties such as strong fluorescence, low cost, non-toxic, and good biocompatibility [37]. It was also reported that CQDs derived from some sources showed peroxidase-like activity which could be used for colorimetric sensing of hydrogen peroxide [38], glucose [39], ascorbic acid [40], and glutathione [41]. The preparation of CQDs with peroxidase-like activity from graphite powder requires a lot of strong oxidants, such as concentrated nitric acid or concentrated sulfuric acid, which are hazardous [38]. The hydrothermal synthesis with natural biomass as carbon source can make up for this defect, and no strong oxidant is needed in the preparation. Compared with graphite powder, natural biomass contains hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and trace amount of sulfur and iron elements. Multielement-doped CQDs with high peroxidase-like activity can be prepared via hydrothermal treatment of natural biomass [39–41]. It is well known that animal blood contains hemoglobin, hormone, choline, sugar, fat and various inorganic salts, which belongs to complex multielement mixture. Considering that chicken blood can be obtained with low cost and sufficient source, CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of chicken blood (Scheme 1 ) in this study. The obtained chicken blood based on CQDs (CB-CQDs) exhibited excellent peroxidase-like activity and could catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2 to form a blue oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) which could be reduced by biothiols causing a blue color fading and an absorbance decreasing. Therefore, a convenient and sensitive colorimetric method for biothiols assay using CB-CQDs as peroxidase mimics was developed.

HcySH, CySH, GSH, and TMB were ordered from Aladdin Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Threonine (Thr), alanine (Ala), isoleucine (Ile), glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), glutamic acid (Glu), tryptophan (Trp), methionine (Met), lysine (Lys), histidine (His), valine (Val), proline (Pro) and tyrosine (Tyr) were obtained from Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd (Guangdong, China). Acetic acid (HAc), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium acetate (NaAc), and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were acquired from Da Mao Chemical Reagents (Tianjin, China). All reagents in experiments were used as received; aqueous solutions were prepared with dual deionized water (DDW).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showing the morphology and size of CQDs was achieved by a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN TEM instrument (FEI Company, the United States) operating at 200 kV. The fluorescence spectra were acquired on a Shimadzu 5301PC fluorospectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CQDs was obtained with Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo, America). Unico 4802 Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China) was used to obtain absorption spectra. Measurements of pH were conducted by using a PXSJ-216 ion analyzer with a combined glass electrode (Shanghai, China). The centrifugation was carried out on an H1850 high speed centrifuge (Changsha, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs from chicken blood
ce:section.ce:para: CB-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of chicken blood. In a typical preparation, 20.0 g of crushed chicken blood was fully mixed with 20.0 mL of DDW under agitation. The suspension was transferred into a 75 mL autoclave and then kept in an oven at 180 °C for a period of 10 h. After cooling, a brown suspension was obtained. The purification of CB-CQDs was conducted by centrifugation at 10,000 rotations per minute for 15 min, followed by dialysis (500–1000 Da dialysis bag) against deionized water for 24 h. Finally, the resulting CB-CQDs solution was diluted to 50 mL with DDW and then kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of peroxidase-like activity of CB-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: For evaluating the peroxidase-like activity of CB-CQDs, catalytic oxidations of TMB with CB-CQDs in the presence of H2O2 are investigated. 0.1 mL CB-CQDs was added into colorimetric tube containing 0.5 mL of TMB (20 mM), 0.5 mL of H2O2 (25 mM), and 3.9 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH4.5). After being fully mixed and incubated at room temperature for 30 min, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. The control experiments were carried out at the same time.

The detection of biothiols was performed as follows: in a series of colorimetric tubes, 0.5 mL of TMB (20 mM), 0.5 mL of H2O2 (25 mM), and 0.1 mL of CB-CQDs were fully mixed in 3.8 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer at pH4.5. Then, various concentrations of biothiols standard solution (0.1 mL) were added into the above mixture. After they were well mixed and incubated at 40 °C for 25 min, the absorption spectra were recorded on a Unico 4802 ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer at room temperature. The calibration curves for biothiols were established according to the decrease of absorbance defined as ΔA= A 0﹣A, where A 0 and A denote the absorbance at 652 nm without and with analyte, individually.

The serum samples were provided by the members of our research group. The Hospital of Guangxi University helped to draw them. The feasibility of this method was verified by standard addition method. To achieve deproteinization, 1.0 mL of serum sample was mixed with 1.0 mL of trichloroacetic acid (5 %) followed by centrifugation for 10 min at 12,000 rotations per minute. The resulting supernatant was 10-fold diluted with DDW to reduce matrix effect. Then, certain amounts of CySH were spiked into the pretreated samples. The measurement of CySH was executed in accordance with the procedure described in Section 2.4, and the contents of CySH were calculated based on the linear equation obtained from CySH standard solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical properties of CB-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As seen in TEM image (Fig. 1 a), the resultant CB-CQDs have a nearly spherical morphology and are individually distributed without aggregation. They exhibit a size range of 1.5–3.6 nm with an average diameter of about 2.4 nm, fitting well to the Gaussian function (Fig. 1b). The HRTEM image displays well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.27 nm (Fig. 1c), which can be attributed to the (020) plane lattice spacing in graphene [42].

Under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light CB-CQDs show a blue fluorescence (inset Fig. 2 a). It was reported that CQDs often exhibit excitation related fluorescence behavior. Thus, the formation of CQDs was further confirmed by fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2a, the maximum emission wavelengths are gradually red-shifted from 430 to 506 nm as the excitation wavelengths increase from 300 to 440 nm, which coincides with those reported in literatures [30,32]. Although the red shift mechanism of the emission wavelength of CQDs needs to be further explored, previous studies have generally confirmed that the excitation dependent luminescence behavior may be related to their size and surface defects [43].

The FTIR spectrum was measured to characterize the functional groups of CB-CQDs. Distinctive bands are illustrated in Fig. 2b, including the stretching vibrations of NH at 390 cm−1, OH at 3250 cm−1, CH at 2880 cm−1, and CO at 1670 cm−1. In addition, the existence of bending vibrations of NH at 1400 cm−1 and CO bond at 1120 cm-1 are observed. The above results shown that the surface of the obtained CQDs were filled with hydrophilic groups (such as OH, NH2 and COOH), which insured CQDs were readily water-dispersible and excellent biocompatibility.

For evaluating the peroxidase-like activity of the prepared CB-CQDs, the controlled experiments were carried out using TMB in HAc–NaAc buffer (pH 4.5) involving either H2O2 only or H2O2 with CB-CQDs. The results are shown in Fig. 3 . TMB solution is colorless, and no characteristic absorption peak is found in the absorption spectrum (curve a). TMB involving H2O2 in the absence of CB-CQDs only leads to a shallower bluish with a very low absorbance at 652 nm (curve b), indicating that H2O2 can cause slow oxidation of TMB. However, TMB containing H2O2 and CB-CQDs leads to the formation of dark blue colored solution with a strong absorbance at 652 nm (curve c), which is similar to the oxidation of TMB catalyzed by horseradish peroxidase (HRP) [44]. These results demonstrate that the prepared CB-CQDs exhibits peroxidase-like activity and can catalyze oxidation of TMB in the presence of H2O2. This may be related to the fact that the CQDs originate from animal blood contains various elements, especially iron [39].

In order to further study the catalytic activity of the CB-CQDs, the steady-state kinetic analysis of CB-CQDs-catalyzed TMB oxidation was performed. The experiments were carried out at room temperature using 0.1 mL of CB-CQDs in 4.9 mL HAc–NaAc buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.5) involving either 5.0 mM H2O2 with TMB concentration in the range of 0.2–1.8 mM or 4.0 mM TMB with H2O2 concentration ranged from 0.25 to 2.0 mM. Absorbance of the resulting solution at 652 nm was recorded at one minute interval. The concentration of oxidation products (ox-TMB) was estimated according to the absorbance and molar extinction coefficient (ε = 3.9 × 104 M−1 cm−1) by using Beer–Lambert law. The initial velocity of reaction was then calculated and plotted against concentration of TMB or H2O2. As shown in Fig. S1a and b, the oxidation reaction catalyzed by CB-CQDs follows the typical Michealis-Menten curve for both substrates TMB and H2O2. Double-reciprocal curves were drawn (Fig. S1c, d) and fitted to the following equation:
(1)
1 V
=
K m
V max
⋅
1
S
+
1
V max

In Eq. (1), V denotes the initial velocity, V max refers to the maximum reaction velocity, [S] represents the concentration of substrate, and K m stands for the Michaelis constant, which is related to the catalytic activity of enzyme. The smaller the K m value is, the stronger the affinity between enzyme and substrate, and the higher the catalytic activity. As summarized in Table S1, the K m value of CB-CQDs towards H2O2 is smaller than those of HRP [45], papain [46] and Co NPs nanoparticles [47], suggesting that lower H2O2 concentration is required for CB-CQDs to reach maximum reaction velocity than that for above catalysts. However, the K m value of CB-CQDs using TMB as the substrate is larger than those of HRP, Mo-CQDs [48] and N-CQDs [40], implying that CB-CQDs exhibits a lower affinity for TMB than HRP, Mo-CQDs and N-CQDs. It is noteworthy that the K m values of CB-CQDs towards TMB and H2O2 are close to N-CQDs, indicating that the obtained CB-CQDs can also be used as an efficient peroxidase mimics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CB-CQDs in biothiols detection
ce:section.ce:para:
As amino acids containing sulfhydryl group, biothiols are highly reductive. Based on the reducibility of biothiols and the peroxidase-like activity of CB-CQDs, colorimetric analysis of biothiols was investigated. As displayed in Fig. 4 , the mixture of TMB + H2O2 + CB-CQDs shows a clear blue color with a strong absorption peak at 652 nm (curve d). However, the addition of biothiols into above mixture leads to a decrease in absorbance and a fading in blue (curve a–c), indicating that a novel biothiols detection method can be established with the prepared CB-CQDs. The determination principle of biothiols is described as follows. Under the catalysis of CB-CQDs, the colorless TMB is oxidized by H2O2 to form blue ox-TMB (Eq. (2)), which can be reduced by biothiols to produce TMB [41] (Eq. (3)), resulting in an absorbance decrease. Thus, we can determinate biothiols by the change of absorbance.
(2)
H 2
O 2
+ TMB
⟶ CB-CQDs
H 2
O+ox-TMB
(3)
ox-TMB + 2 BSH → TMB+BSSB+2
H +
B = Cy, GS, HCy

In order to achieve the best response, several factors, such as pH value, incubation temperature, incubation time, and the concentrations of CB-CQDs, H2O2, TMB, which may influence the change of absorbance, were optimized by taking CySH as an example and using ΔA as optimization criterion.

The oxidation and reduction of TMB are usually carried out in weak acid medium. Consequently, the influence of pH was studied by using HAc-NaAc buffer with pH value ranging from 3.0–5.5. It is found that the ΔA reaches the maximum at pH 4.5, and beyond this pH, ΔA starts to decrease (Fig. S2). This is similar to other nanomaterials with peroxidase-like activity [49–51]. Thus, pH 4.5 was adopted for the next experiments. The influence of incubation temperature on ΔA was investigated in the range of 20∼60 °C (Fig. S3). At low temperature (20∼40 °C), the slow reaction speed leads to the low value of ΔA. On the other hand, too high incubation temperature (60 °C) causes H2O2 decomposition and incomplete oxidation of TMB, resulting in no obvious color change with a low value of ΔA. It is clear that the best signal response is obtained at the incubation temperature of 40∼50 °C. In this work, 40 °C was selected as the incubation temperature. The effect of different incubation time (5∼30 min) on ΔA was evaluated. As observed in Fig. S4, with the increase of incubation time, the ΔA value increases gradually. Whereas the incubation time exceeds 20 min, the ΔA value remains unchanged. Therefore, 25 min was conservatively chosen as the best incubation time. The influences of catalyst dosage and substrate concentration on ΔA were also examined by changing the dosage of CB-CQDs and the concentration of TMB and H2O2, and the results are presented in Fig. S5−7. As seen, the maximum ΔA is obtained at 100 μL of CB-CQDs, 2 mM TMB and 2.5 mM H2O2, which were adopted for the further experiments.

The selectivity of the proposed method for the determination of biothiols was examined. Various common amino acids, including Met, Ser, His, Trp, Lys, Pro, Thr, Glu, Val, Ala, Tyr, Gly, and Ile, were added to TMB solution containing H2O2 and CB-CQDs separately. The mixtures were then treated according to the procedure described in Section 2.4, and the results are presented in Fig. 6 . It is obvious that ΔA value in the presence of biothiols is significantly higher than that of other amino acids although the concentration of these amino acids is 100 folds that of biothiols. These results demonstrate that the present method possesses an excellent selectivity toward biothiols detection.

To validate the practicability of the proposed method, a standard addition method was adopted for the measurement of CySH in real samples, and two parallel experiments were conducted on each sample. The results in Table 2 indicate that the spiked and the measured values are in good agreement with each other and the average recoveries are in the range of 91.5%–109.3% with RSD between 1.0 % and 4.3 %, demonstrating an accurate and dependable method.

In this work, CB-CQDs emitting blue fluorescence were prepared from chicken blood, which exhibited excellent optical properties and could be used as a peroxidase mimics to catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to generate blue ox-TMB. The ox-TMB could be further reduced by biothiols leading to a blue color fading and an absorbance decrease. Based on this, we established a convenient and sensitive colorimetric method for biothiols assay using CB-CQDs as peroxidase mimics. The LODs of CySH, GSH and HcySH detection were found to be 0.4, 0.6, and 0.9 μM, respectively. Compared with natural enzymes, CB-CQDs as peroxidase mimetic has the advantages of low cost, good stability and resistant inactivation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (21567002), the Dean Project of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Biorefinery (GXKLB-201801) and the Foundation of College Student Innovation Ability Training of Guangxi University (201910593179).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112529.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-isophorone fluorophore-based design of a ratiometric fluorescent probe and its application in the sensing of biothiols
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Wang, Y. Wang, G. Wang, S. Chen,C. Huang, Two-isophorone fluorophore-based design of a ratiometric fluorescent probe and its application in the sensing of biothiols, J. Mater. Chem. B 7 (2019) 5633-5639, https://doi.org/ 10.1039/C9TB01671F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-site fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of sulfite and biothiols
Chin. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Li, P. Cui, K. Li, J. Feng, M. Zou,X. Yu, Dual-site fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of sulfite and biothiols, Chinese Chem. Lett. 29 (2018) 992-994, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cclet.2017.11.011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitroolefin-modified cyclometalated iridium(iii) complexes for tunable detection of biothiols with deep-red emission
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhao, Y. Pan, X. Wang, X.-W. Gao, D. Chao, Nitroolefin-modified cyclometalated iridium(iii) complexes for tunable detection of biothiols with deep-red emission, New J. Chem. 42 (2018) 5508-5513, https://doi.org/ 10.1039/C8NJ00314A.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modulating DNA-templated silver nanoclusters for fluorescence turn-on detection of thiol compounds
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Huang, F. Pu, Y. Lin, J. Ren,X. Qu, Modulating DNA-templated silver nanoclusters for fluorescence turn-on detection of thiol compounds, Chem. Commun. 47 (2011) 3487-9, https://doi.org/ 10.1039/C0CC05651K.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-site fluorescent probe for visualizing the metabolism of Cys in living cells
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yue, F. Huo, P. Ning, Y. Zhang, J. Chao, X. Meng,C. Yin, Dual-Site Fluorescent Probe for Visualizing the Metabolism of Cys in Living Cells, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 139 (2017) 3181-3185, https://doi.org/ 10.1021/jacs.6b12845.

Talanta

Sens. Actuators B

B. 280 (2019) 62-68, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.029.

Talanta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-selective fluorescent probe for discriminating glutathione and homocysteine simultaneously
Spectrochim. Acta A
